title: Mercedes F1 Wheel — Walkthrough
sourcePrompt: Walk me through this Mercedes-AMG F1 steering wheel.

sourceInputs.title: Why this wheel is its own computer
What this CAD does NOT model

spatialElements.title: Wheel Identity

facts.value: Carbon-fibre chassis, suede grips, magnesium quick-release

interactions.behavior: expand this panel to a fuller view

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: Short readable text — a 2D panel keeps it scannable without occupying physical workspace.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Persistent reference content (status, insurance, history) belongs to the user's left so it stays glanceable without competing with the repair target straight ahead.

spatialElements.title: Why this wheel is its own computer

sourceContent.title: Why this wheel is its own computer
sourceContent.body: An F1 wheel is the driver's primary interface to a ~$15M race car. It carries the dash display, every in-race adjustment (brake bias, differential, fuel mix, ERS deployment, torque maps, DRS, pit limiter), the radio, and the clutch + shift mechanics. The driver makes 50-100 mid-lap inputs without taking their hands off the grips.

interactions.behavior: expand this panel to a fuller view

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: Short readable text — a 2D panel keeps it scannable without occupying physical workspace.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Persistent reference content (status, insurance, history) belongs to the user's left so it stays glanceable without competing with the repair target straight ahead.

spatialElements.title: Race-day adjustments

sourceContent.title: Race-day adjustments
items: Brake bias — rotary, shifts F/R brake pressure split
Differential — rotary, locks/opens the diff for corner entry / mid / exit
Fuel mix — multifunction switch, lean ↔ rich
Torque map — multifunction, engine character per stint
ERS deployment — paddle / button, harvests + deploys battery
DRS — button or paddle, opens rear wing on straights
Pit limiter — button, caps speed in pit lane
Drink — button, releases water from helmet bladder
Radio — button, talks to the pit wall

interactions.behavior: expand this panel to a fuller view

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A list is read top-to-bottom — a vertical 2D panel beats any 3D arrangement.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Active instructions (steps, tools list) live to the user's right — the dominant working side — so they stay in peripheral view while the user looks at the target.

spatialElements.title: What this CAD does NOT model

sourceContent.title: What this CAD does NOT model
items: Individual button surfaces (the real wheel has ~25 buttons; here they are decals on a flat plastic face)
LED rev strip across the top
Carbon-fibre weave detail (treated as plain matte black plastic for v0.1 visualisation)

interactions.behavior: expand this panel to a fuller view

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A list is read top-to-bottom — a vertical 2D panel beats any 3D arrangement.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Active instructions (steps, tools list) live to the user's right — the dominant working side — so they stay in peripheral view while the user looks at the target.

spatialElements.title: Mercedes F1 Steering Wheel

sourceContent.name: Mercedes F1 Steering Wheel
sourceContent.finding: The centrepiece. Sits on the workbench so the driver-controls layout is readable from the user's seat. Bright translucent shader is the spatial-language signal that this is the subject of every other element in the scene.

interactions.behavior: tint the target with the bright blue shader
hide every other element so only the target remains
show a fuller label panel for this target

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: The user is acting on a real / simulated object — emphasize it in place with a bright shader so the serviced part is unmistakable, rather than describing it in text.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Hero object sits on a clean workbench — no host vehicle to anchor to, so the user can walk around the whole part for inspection.

spatialElements.title: Left Grip Handle

sourceContent.name: Left Grip Handle
sourceContent.finding: Suede-wrapped handle the driver's left hand never leaves. Behind it: the left paddle (downshift) and the left clutch paddle (bite-point control on race starts).

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Right Grip Handle

sourceContent.name: Right Grip Handle
sourceContent.finding: Right-hand grip. Behind it: the right paddle (upshift) and the right clutch paddle. On many seasons the DRS button also lives on the back of this grip.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Center Console / Display Bay

sourceContent.name: Center Console / Display Bay
sourceContent.finding: Houses the LCD dash readout (gear, speed, lap delta, tyre temps, fuel, ERS state). Surrounded by the most frequently-used buttons: radio, OK / confirm, drink, BB+ / BB-.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Top-Left Control Bay

sourceContent.name: Top-Left Control Bay
sourceContent.finding: On most Mercedes wheels this carries the brake-bias rotary and the clutch-bite rotary. Brake bias is adjusted into and out of slow corners; bite-point is set on the formation lap.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Top-Right Control Bay

sourceContent.name: Top-Right Control Bay
sourceContent.finding: Differential rotary + strategy multifunction. Diff entry / mid / exit settings get touched on every lap. Strategy mode swaps the whole map set: race / qualifying / safety car / cool-down.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Mercedes Star — Quick-Release Nut

sourceContent.name: Mercedes Star — Quick-Release Nut
sourceContent.finding: The team logo is the visible face of the quick-release collar. Pull, the wheel comes off in ~1 second — required by FIA regulation so a driver can egress in <5 seconds.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Lower Rim Bar

sourceContent.name: Lower Rim Bar
sourceContent.finding: The structural cross-piece that joins the left and right grip handles into one composite chassis. Typically holds the pit-limiter button + the burnout / start-mode trigger in a centre position.

interactions.behavior: show fuller detail about this anchored marker

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A physical interaction point (clip, screw, port) only makes sense when pinned directly to the part it sits on.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Physical interaction points (clips, screws, ports) only make sense pinned directly on the target — the label sits *on* the part it names.

spatialElements.title: Rotaries change car behaviour mid-corner

sourceContent.title: Rotaries change car behaviour mid-corner
sourceContent.finding: Every rotary affects the chassis dynamic. A mis-clicked differential setting on corner entry can rotate the car off line. Drivers train these positions by feel — that is why the labels are recessed into the chassis, not raised: the finger reads the click, not the eyes.

interactions.behavior: expand the diagnostic finding into a fuller panel

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A diagnosis explains *why* the user is here — float it above the target so it reads as commentary on the physical part, not as another anchored sticker.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: A diagnosis explains *why* the user is here — floated above the target so it reads as commentary on the physical part rather than as another sticker on it.

spatialElements.title: Explore by region

options.label: Focus on grips + paddles
options.detail: Walk the user through the left/right handles and the paddles immediately behind them.

options.label: Focus on top control bays
options.detail: Brake bias, differential, strategy, clutch bite — the rotary cluster region.

options.label: Focus on the dash
options.detail: What the LCD shows during a lap; the buttons around it that the driver hits without looking.

options.label: Focus on the quick-release
options.detail: Explain the 1-second wheel removal and FIA egress timing rule.

interactions.behavior: pick this option and advance the experience

spatialElements.whyThisRepresentation: A small set of next-actions belongs in the user's hand-reach zone, not on a wall — floating selectable cards.
spatialElements.whyThisPlacement: Decisions need to be touched / selected, so they live in the user's hand-reach zone, not on a distant wall.

reasoningSummary: Prompt "Walk me through this Mercedes-AMG F1 steering wheel." was understood as domain "motorsport_engineering" with 14 content items. Spatial mix: 4× two_d_panel, 1× highlighted_target, 7× anchored_callout, 1× diagnostic_overlay, 1× floating_decision_card. Textual and numeric content stayed as readable panels rather than being forced into 3D widgets. Diagnoses were floated above the diagnosed part as commentary, not stickers on it. Decisions were placed in the user's hand-reach zone for direct selection.
